source.title: The locus coeruleus-noradrenergic system: modulation of behavioral state and state-dependent cognitive processes

confidence_rationale: This is the canonical comprehensive review of the LC-NE system's role in behavioral state regulation. It directly establishes that the LC-NE system contributes to initiation and maintenance of waking behavioral and forebrain activity states, and that LC dysregulation contributes to arousal and sleep disorders. The review explicitly situates LC-NE modulation within an inverted-U framework where both too little and too much NE impairs performance. Crucially for MECH-178, the review documents LC-NE's role in maintaining arousal states that are incompatible with REM -- i.e., that tonic LC activity above baseline prevents entry into REM. Clinical relevance is addressed directly with reference to PTSD and affective disorders. Confidence 0.70 because this is a review synthesizing pre-2003 evidence: it predates optogenetic causal confirmation of the LC->REM-suppression link, and does not address chronic pharmacological elevation (NRIs) specifically.
failure_signatures: As a 2003 review, it predates optogenetic and chemogenetic studies that provided causal confirmation of LC activity's role in REM suppression -- the evidence base is correlational and pharmacological.
Does not specifically address NRI antidepressants or stimulants as sources of tonic LC elevation relevant to MECH-178's pharmacological claims.
The inverted-U framework for NE-cognitive performance does not directly address the sleep-deprivation mechanism for behavioural rigidity proposed by MECH-178.

mapping.source_claim_statement: The LC-NE system contributes to the initiation and maintenance of behavioral and forebrain activity states appropriate for sensory information collection (waking). Dysregulation of LC-NE neurotransmission may contribute to cognitive and arousal dysfunction in sleep/arousal disorders, ADHD, PTSD, and affective disorders. NE modulation of synaptic strength and cognitive state is state-dependent.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-178 claims that elevated tonic LC-NE activity -- whether from chronic stress, stimulants, or NRIs -- maintains LC firing above the silence threshold required for REM generation, thereby blocking MECH-123 precision recalibration. Berridge & Waterhouse 2003 directly supports the mechanistic logic: (1) the LC-NE system is a primary driver of arousal states incompatible with REM; (2) dysregulation of LC-NE is implicated in sleep and arousal disorders; and (3) excessive NE activity is associated with cognitive inflexibility (though the pathway described is waking-state NE excess, not REM deprivation). This review provides the broader mechanistic context that grounds MECH-178's LC-to-REM-suppression inference.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Berridge & Waterhouse frame LC-NE dysregulation and cognitive effects primarily in terms of waking NE modulation, not specifically through sleep architecture disruption. The MECH-178-specific prediction -- that it is the REM-suppression pathway (not waking LC excess) that produces the behavioural rigidity -- is a specific mechanistic sub-claim not directly tested or endorsed by this review.
mapping.source_context: Comprehensive 2003 review in Brain Research Reviews (Brain Res Brain Res Rev 42:33-84) from University of Wisconsin. Covers LC anatomy, physiology, neurochemistry, and behavioral/cognitive function. One of the most-cited reviews on the LC-NE system. Berridge has been a leading figure in LC-NE research for three decades.

confidence_components.notes: Very high source quality -- seminal, comprehensive review from leading experts, widely cited. Mapping fidelity is moderate: the review strongly supports the upstream mechanistic components of MECH-178 (LC drives arousal/waking incompatible with REM; LC dysregulation produces cognitive dysfunction) but does not directly test or confirm the specific sleep-mediated rigidity pathway. Transfer risk is low as this is a broad mechanistic review applicable to both rodent and human physiology.
